Final Project Executive Summary

# Introduction

New York City is one of the largest energy consumers in the United
States, with its building stock accounting for approximately 70% of the
city's total greenhouse gas emissions. Understanding what drives energy
consumption in buildings is critical for informing policy decisions,
guiding retrofits, and achieving the city's climate goals under Local
Law 97, which mandates significant emissions reductions from large
buildings by 2030.

The prediction research question for this project is: Can we predict a
building's Site Energy Use Intensity (kBtu/ft²) based on its physical
and operational characteristics? This project aims to predict a
building's Site Energy Use Intensity (Site EUI, measured in kBtu/ft²),
which represents the amount of energy a building consumes per square
foot per year. Site EUI is a widely used benchmarking metric because it
normalizes energy consumption by building size, allowing fair
comparisons across buildings of different scales. A lower Site EUI
indicates a more energy-efficient building. Since Site EUI is a
continuous numeric variable, this is a regression problem.

Accurately predicting Site EUI based on a building's physical and
operational characteristics, such as size, age, and occupancy type, is
highly valuable for multiple stakeholders. Building owners and property
managers can use such a model to identify cost-saving opportunities and
prioritize energy retrofits. Policymakers can use findings about which
features are most predictive of high energy use to guide future building
codes and enforcement strategies. More broadly, this project contributes
to ongoing research on urban energy consumption and climate
accountability.

# Data Source

The data used in this project comes from NYC Open Data's Energy and
Water Data Disclosure for Local Law 84 (2021), which contains energy
benchmarking reports submitted annually by owners of large NYC
buildings. The dataset contains over 25,000 observations and includes
variables describing building characteristics, location, occupancy, and
energy consumption metrics.

NYC Open Data. (2021). Energy and Water Data Disclosure for Local Law 84
2021 (Data for Calendar Year 2020). City of New York.
https://data.cityofnewyork.us/Environment/Energy-and-Water-Data-Disclosure-for-Local-Law-84-/usc3-8zwd/about_data

# Data Overview

Data Quality & Missingness The raw dataset contains 28,067 observations
and 250 variables, of which 19 are numeric and 231 are categorical. A
missingness inspection revealed that several variables have notable
proportions of missing values. For numeric variables, missing values
will be imputed using the median to avoid sensitivity to outliers. For
categorical variables, rows with missing values will be removed. Given
the large number of observations, this approach is unlikely to
meaningfully skew model estimates.

Target Variable: Site EUI The response variable, Site EUI (kBtu/ft²),
was heavily right-skewed in its raw form, with a small number of
buildings reporting extremely high energy use intensity — likely large
or industrial facilities with outsized consumption. To address this, a
log transformation (log1p) was applied, resulting in a more symmetric,
approximately normal distribution centered around 90 kBtu/ft². This
transformation stabilizes variance and improves model performance for
algorithms sensitive to distributional assumptions. Outliers on the
higher end of the distribution are expected given the nature of the data
and will be accounted for during feature engineering and model building.

# Methods

Data Splitting The dataset was split into training and testing sets
using an 80/20 split, with 80% of observations allocated to training and
20% reserved for final model evaluation. Stratified sampling on the
target variable site_eui was used to ensure both sets had a similar
distribution of energy use intensity values. This is a regression
prediction problem, as the goal is to predict a continuous numeric
outcome.

Resampling To evaluate model performance during tuning and selection,
5-fold cross-validation with 4 repeats was applied to the training set,
again stratified by site_eui. This resulted in 20 total resamples,
providing a robust and stable estimate of out-of-sample performance
while reducing the risk of overfitting to any single fold.

Recipes Recipe 1 Main included the full set of predictors after
cleaning, with standard preprocessing steps including imputation of
missing values, dummy encoding of categorical variables, and
normalization of numeric predictors where required.

Recipe 2 Main was a variation designed to explore the predictive
importance of certain variable groups — notably energy use variables
(electricity, natural gas, steam, etc.) and property-type floor area
variables. This recipe has a higher chance of being successful due to
it's specific tailoring.

These things hold true for Recipe 1 Tree and Recipe 2 Tree.

# Model Types

7 model types were fit and compared:

Null Model — a baseline that predicts the mean of site_eui for all
observations, serving as a performance floor Baseline Model — a simple
reference model used to contextualize the performance of more complex
models Linear Regression (LM) — a parametric model that assumes a linear
relationship between predictors and the outcome Elastic Net (EN) — a
regularized regression model combining L1 and L2 penalties, useful for
handling correlated predictors and performing variable selection
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) — a non-parametric model that predicts based
on the average outcome of the most similar observations in the training
set Random Forest (RF) — an ensemble of decision trees using bagging,
which captures non-linear relationships and interaction effects Boosted
Tree (BT/XGBoost) — a sequential ensemble method that iteratively fits
trees to residuals, offering strong predictive performance on structured
tabular data.

# Tuning Parameters

In order to determine tuning parameters for each model I tuned broadly
without any set parameters analyzed the autoplots for the results. I
continued to change the parameters and narrow down what would be the
best parameters and the parameters showed in the models are the most
efficient best parameters. For some models I had to analyze 2 or 3
different autoplots however the ones uploaded are the best and final
autoplots.

KNN — tuned neighbors. Recipe 1 optimal at k ≈ 13–15, Recipe 2 at k ≈
30–40, both showing classic U-shape. Elastic Net — tuned penalty and
mixture. Recipe 1 preferred heavy regularization (log penalty ≈ 1.75),
Recipe 2 preferred low penalty with mixture ≈ 0.2–0.3, suggesting the
two feature sets respond very differently to regularization. Boosted
Tree — tuned tree_depth and min_n. Both recipes agreed on depth 8–9 and
min_n ≈ 23–24 (RMSE ≈ 44–45). Random Forest — tuned mtry and min_n, with
trees fixed at 150. Both recipes identified mtry = 8 and min_n ≈ 4–5 as
optimal RMSE ≈ 54–55.

# Model Selection Metric

To compare models and select a final one, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
was used as the primary evaluation metric. RMSE measures the average
magnitude of prediction errors in the same units as the outcome variable
site_eui, making it straightforward to interpret. Lower RMSE means that
the model's predictions are closer to the actual energy use values on
average. RMSE was preferred over Mean Absolute Error (MAE) because it
squares the errors before averaging, which means larger mistakes are
penalized more heavily. This felt appropriate for this problem since
significantly over- or under-predicting a building's energy would be a
problem.

# Final Model

The best performing model overall is Boosted Tree (r2) with the lowest
RMSE of 44.29. So from fitting this model, the winning Boosted Tree
model achieves an RMSE of 34.8, R-squared of 0.675, and MAE of 9.77 on
the test data — notably, the RMSE of 34.8 is a significant improvement
over the tuning RMSE of 44.29, suggesting the model generalizes well and
wasn't overfit. The null baseline had an RMSE of ~69.77, so the Boosted
Tree cuts error roughly in half, which is a meaningful.The R-squared of
0.675 means the model explains about 67.5% of variance in the outcome,
which is reasonably strong.

The bulk of predictions cluster tightly along the red dashed
perfect-prediction line at lower values (0–300), indicating the model
performs well for typical observations.However due to outliers, the
model struggles at the extremes of the distribution.

Boosted Trees naturally handle nonlinearity and complex interactions
between features without requiring manual feature engineering, which
likely explains why it outperformed other models.

# Comment on Generative AI Use

I used Generative AI to help organize my ideas and to explain my
reasoning for my metrics as well as for tuning parameters. I used AI to
help me analyze the autoplots for each model so that the parameters used
for each model were as accurate as possible. In addition, I used it for
the explanation for each model and the EDA.

# Appendix: Tuning Parameter Analysis

Tuning Parameters Analysis of Autoplots

EN plots autoen1 shows RMSE improving (dropping from \~76 to \~70) as
regularization increases, then flattening out, with the lasso proportion
having little effect. autoen2 shows the opposite pattern, starting at a
better RMSE of \~66 with low regularization and worsening as
regularization increases, making the 2nd recipe the overall
better-performing recipe out of the two.

KNN plots autoknn1 shows a clear U-shape with RMSE bottoming out at
\~62.1 around k=13–15 neighbors before rising steadily to \~62.6, making
that the optimal k. autoknn2 shows a steep decline from \~67 at k=1 that
flattens into a broad minimum of \~64.1 around k=30–40, with a much
wider range of acceptable k values but an overall worse best RMSE
compared to r1.

Appendix EDA: Distribution of Site EUI Plots(site_eui_histogram_log &
site_eui_histogram) Transforming to log scale reveals the underlying
shape much more clearly: the distribution becomes approximately normal,
centered around 80–120 kBtu/ft², with a gentle taper on both sides.
Better for linear models.

Top Predictors Electricity Use (Grid Purchase) shows a weak positive
relationship with a flattening loess curve, huge variance, suggesting
electricity explains some but not all of the EUI story.

Natural Gas Use has a striking inverted-U loess curve, peaking around
1.5×10⁹ kBtu before declining. Buildings with extreme gas use may be
large industrial or specialty properties where floor area dominates the
denominator of EUI. This variable may need to be removed.

Hospital Floor Area and Laboratory Floor Area both show the bulk of data
jammed at zero with a few extreme outliers.

Occupancy shows discrete clustering suggesting it may be ordinal or
bucketed rather than continuous. The flat loess confirms weak direct
linear correlation with EUI.

Supermarket/Grocery Floor Area mirrors the hospital/lab pattern highly
sparse with most buildings at zero.

Overall, the extreme outlier points visible across all panels suggest
that outlier treatment and log transformation of predictors will be
critical before fitting any model.

Site Eui by Borough The median EUI is remarkably consistent across all
five boroughs, all sitting around 50–150 kBtu/ft². However, Manhattan
stands out with the single most extreme outlier (\~6,800 kBtu/ft²),
which likely corresponds to a hospital, data center, or lab. Queens also
shows a notable high outlier (\~1,200). Staten Island has fewer extreme
values and a higher median. Borough alone is probably not a strong
standalone predictor.

Median Site EUI by Property The spread across property types has a large
range so property type should be a primary feature in any
predictive model.
